does: This paper introduces JailbreakLens, an interpretability framework that analyzes how jailbreak attacks succeed against LLMs by examining both representational and circuit-level mechanisms. The method investigates how adversarial prompts shift the model's internal representations of harmfulness and identifies specific circuits responsible for safety failures. Rather than focusing on single jailbreak patterns, JailbreakLens provides fine-grained explanations across diverse jailbreak strategies, revealing how prompts manipulate the model's understanding of what constitutes harmful content and which computational pathways they exploit. The framework bridges the gap between high-level behavioral changes (refusal rates) and the low-level mechanistic failures that enable jailbreaks, offering a comprehensive view of vulnerability across different attack types and model components.
connects: This work extends recent mechanistic interpretability research that has analyzed LLM vulnerabilities through representation analysis and neuron-level attribution methods. It builds on earlier studies examining how jailbreak prompts cause representation shifts in latent space and identifies safety-critical neurons, but addresses their limitations by analyzing diverse jailbreak patterns rather than isolated examples. The paper connects to broader circuits-based interpretability research that traces computational mechanisms through neural networks, adapting these techniques to the safety domain. It also relates to work on understanding refusal mechanisms in LLMs and how adversarial inputs can bypass them, while contributing novel insights about the relationship between representational changes and circuit failures in enabling jailbreaks.
enables: JailbreakLens opens pathways for developing more robust safety mechanisms by revealing the specific representational and computational vulnerabilities that jailbreaks exploit. Understanding these mechanisms enables targeted defense strategies that can harden critical circuits or add redundant safety representations. The framework supports future research into circuit-level safety interventions and could help researchers design better alignment techniques that address fundamental computational vulnerabilities rather than treating symptoms. It also provides tools for systematically evaluating new jailbreak attempts and predicting vulnerability patterns. Furthermore, the methodology can generalize to other forms of adversarial attacks on language models, potentially advancing broader mechanistic safety research and contributing to more interpretable, controllable AI systems.
